---
title: "Path to Game Development"
subtitle: "The mathematics that turns code into worlds"
---

## What you can do at the end of this path

- Implement a 3D rendering pipeline from scratch — vectors, matrices,
  projections, quaternions, lighting.
- Reason about physics simulation: integration of motion, collision response,
  constraints.
- Read graphics-programming texts (Real-Time Rendering, PBR Book) without the
  maths blocking you.

## The path

Game dev needs geometry first, then trigonometry, then linear algebra in 3D,
then calculus for physics, then differential geometry for advanced surfaces
and animation.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
    A[Tier 1<br/>Shapes, area, multiplication-as-area] --> B[Tier 2<br/>Pythagoras, similarity, scale]
    B --> C[Tier 3<br/>Trigonometry, conics]
    C --> D[Tier 4<br/>Vectors in 2D and 3D, calculus]
    D --> E[Tier 5<br/>Linear algebra<br/>Multivariable calculus]
    E --> F[Tier 6<br/>ODEs/PDEs, numerical methods]
    F --> G[Tier 7 — Game Dev track:<br/>Differential geometry<br/>Quaternions<br/>Computational geometry]

    classDef built fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
    class A built
```

## Where to start right now

The game dev path begins with **Multiplication as Area** in Tier 1 (the
simplest grid is the seed of every texture and pixel array) and **Shapes &
Angles**. Walk Tier 1 in order — the geometry strand is the spine of
everything later.

## What's built / what's planned

- ✅ Tier 1: *What is a Number?* (exemplar lesson)
- 📋 Tier 1 geometry strand and beyond — planned, not yet authored.
